reasoning: The Lean formalization adds two extra hypotheses that do not appear in the informal statement: `¬Subgroup.closure {g} = ⊤` and `¬Subgroup.closure {h} = ⊤` (neither g nor h alone generates G). The informal statement only requires that G is generated by g and h (i.e., ⟨g,h⟩ = G), without any minimality condition. These extra hypotheses are not needed for the claim to be true — e.g., if G = ℤ/3 with g a generator and h = e, both ⟨g,h⟩ = G and the conclusion hold (every element is expressible as a product of elements of S = {g, g⁻¹}). Similarly, in the trivial group case, the informal claim holds but the Lean hypotheses exclude it. Since the Lean version proves the conclusion under strictly more restrictive premises, it is logically weaker than the informal statement. The rest of the formalization (the set S encoding {g^±¹h^±¹}, the list-product form, the bounds on r) correctly captures the informal content.
